metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `_call_with_backoff` is actually called anywhere on the REST or subprocess path (current grep finds zero call sites); if it is dead code, remove the 'ALREADY reads exc.headers … for the REST-style path' premise and redesign the subprocess 429 backoff as new work rather than a 'parity' wire-up.
findings.finding: `_call_with_backoff` is asserted to 'ALREADY' handle the REST-style path with `exc.headers` / exponential backoff, but it has zero call sites in the entire codebase — it is dead/orphaned code, not an active mechanism on any path.
findings.location: Context / corrected premise section: 'the existing `_call_with_backoff` (`acli_subprocess.py:236-292`) ALREADY reads `exc.headers` / does exponential backoff at ~line 267 for the REST-style path'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the existing `_call_with_backoff` ALREADY … for the REST-style path' with an accurate description: '`_call_with_backoff` exists in `acli_subprocess.py` but is currently uncalled (dead code). The subprocess 429 handling must be implemented as new logic directly in `_run_acli`, detecting exit-code-429 signals in `CalledProcessError`, extracting `Retry-After` from stderr via regex if available, and applying jittered exponential backoff — it cannot reuse `_call_with_backoff` which targets `urllib.error.HTTPError` not `subprocess.CalledProcessError`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing new inline backoff in `_run_acli`, evaluate whether `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:236-292) — already a general `fn: Any` wrapper in the same file — can be extended or refactored to cover the subprocess exit-code path, avoiding duplication of backoff constants, Retry-After parsing, and jitter logic.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding jittered exponential backoff + Retry-After parsing inline in `_run_acli`, rebuilding logic that `_call_with_backoff` (same file, acli_subprocess.py:236-292) already provides as a general `fn: Any` wrapper with the identical: 2^(attempt+1)-capped-at-60s delay, 0-1s random jitter, `Retry-After` header float-parse + fallback (lines 264-276), and `_RETRYABLE_HTTP_CODES` {429,502,503} (line 112). The plan never evaluates whether `_call_with_backoff` could wrap `_run_acli`'s inner per-attempt logic instead.
findings.location: What/files section, Step 2; also AC line 2 ('429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff; honors Retry-After IFF...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `subprocess_exit_429` fast-path inside `_call_with_backoff` (or extract a shared `_compute_backoff_delay(attempt, retry_after)` helper), then have `_run_acli` call into that shared helper when it detects a 429-equivalent exit code. This keeps the delay arithmetic, Retry-After float-parse, and retryable-code set in one place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Step 1 probe to explicitly determine whether the ACLI Go binary handles HTTP 429 internally (transparent retry before Python sees any non-zero exit) — e.g., by watching network traffic or injecting a mock server that returns 429 exactly N times and verifying Python observes a CalledProcessError vs. a success.
findings.finding: The probe in Step 1 does not explicitly check whether the ACLI Go binary handles HTTP 429 responses internally (transparent retry/backoff within the binary) before ever propagating a non-zero exit to Python. If ACLI absorbs 429s internally and returns success or a timeout instead of a CalledProcessError, the entire plan premise — Python-level 429 detection and Retry-After parsing from stderr — is vacuous. This is a distinct, unverified capability surface that is load-bearing for the plan's feasibility.
findings.location: What / files — Step 1 (PROBE FIRST): 'run `acli jira workitem search` against a rate-limited/staged endpoint … record BOTH (a) whether ACLI surfaces `Retry-After` in parseable stderr, AND (b) what EXIT CODE ACLI returns on an HTTP 429'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third probe question to Step 1: '(c) does ACLI transparently retry 429s internally before surfacing a non-zero exit to Python? (verify via mock server that returns 429 exactly once, then 200 — if Python sees exit 0, ACLI has internal retry logic)'. Record this result in AC1 alongside (a) and (b).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum retry count and/or total backoff ceiling (e.g., max 5 attempts, cap per-sleep to N seconds) for the new 429 jittered-backoff path, and document whether an oversized Retry-After value is clamped to a maximum.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'jittered exponential backoff' for the 429 path but does not define a maximum retry count or total backoff ceiling. If ACLI returns continuous 429s, the reconciler subprocess could block indefinitely, starving other reconciliation work.
findings.location: What/files section (item 2) and AC item: '429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC item 2: 'Backoff is bounded: max N retries (recommend 5) and per-sleep capped at M seconds (recommend 60); a Retry-After value exceeding M is clamped. After exhausting retries, raise the existing rate-limit error so the caller can fail fast rather than block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 3) requiring a regression test that non-429/non-zero exit-code retries still fire correctly when the add-on path is implemented.
findings.finding: The recommended 'add-on' path (keep all non-zero retries, add 429 handling on top) has no AC verifying that existing non-429 retry behavior is preserved after the change; AC 3 only mandates a test 'if narrowed,' leaving the recommended path unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 3: 'If add-on: a test confirms existing non-zero-exit-code retry behavior is unchanged. If narrowed: a test covers the removed non-429 exit-code behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC2, e.g. a pytest invocation targeting a new test in test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py that injects a synthetic 429 stderr with 'Retry-After: 5' and asserts sleep was called with a value near 5, AND a second test with no Retry-After header asserting jittered exponential backoff (sleep called with a value in the expected range).
findings.finding: No proving command exists for AC2 (jittered exponential backoff + conditional Retry-After), so there is no way to mechanically verify the core deliverable was implemented correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in step 3 whether 429-specific Retry-After/jitter backoff REPLACES the existing uniform `time.sleep(2**(attempt+1))` for 429 cases within the `_run_acli` loop, or whether both sleeps execute — and update AC 2 ('429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff') to assert the expected single-sleep control flow per attempt.
findings.finding: The plan's 'add-on vs narrow' decision for the `_run_acli` retry loop is ambiguous about whether 429-specific backoff REPLACES or SUPPLEMENTS the existing uniform `time.sleep(2**(attempt+1))` on line 430. In the current single loop, a non-zero returncode always executes the uniform sleep and `continue`s. If a 429 branch is inserted without removing the uniform sleep for that case, both sleeps fire — doubling the delay. If the uniform sleep is removed for 429 (replacement), that is a subtle control-flow change the plan doesn't specify. The AC ('add-on vs narrow') describes the scope of which exit codes trigger retries, not the per-attempt sleep interaction, so this stays unresolved for the implementer.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to step 3: 'For 429 (or the probed rate-limit exit code), the 429-specific delay REPLACES the existing uniform sleep for that attempt — not supplements it. Refactor the delay calculation into a helper (e.g. `_compute_retry_delay(attempt, returncode, stderr)`) that returns the Retry-After value if available for 429, otherwise the jittered exponential value, and call it once per retry iteration.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `_call_with_backoff` is (or is intended to be) a live production call path, not dead code; if it is dead, update the plan's 'behavior parity' framing in Step 2 to reference the actual active retry path (`_run_acli`'s internal loop) so the comparison baseline is grounded.
findings.finding: _call_with_backoff is defined in acli_subprocess.py and re-exported via acli.py, but is never called in any production src/ code — it is dead code exercised only in test_error_taxonomy.py. The plan's premise that it is an 'existing REST-style path' that is actively in use is inaccurate; no production caller will be implicitly affected by changes to it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `acli.py` and `acli_cli_ops.py` to the edit-set or explicitly confirm the change is purely internal to `_run_acli`'s loop body (no signature change, no new exception types) so call-site impact at the 9 existing `_run_acli` invocations is addressed.
findings.finding: The plan names only `acli_subprocess.py` as the edit target but does not enumerate `acli_cli_ops.py` (6 direct `acli_subprocess._run_acli(...)` call sites) and `acli.py` (3 direct `acli_subprocess._run_acli(...)` call sites) as consumers. While these are in the same package directory, they are distinct modules that own the `retry_on_timeout` flag decisions and call-site contracts for `_run_acli`; a signature change or new per-call parameter would require updating all nine call sites.
findings.suggested_fix: If the 429 handling is internal to _run_acli (detecting exit code in the existing CalledProcessError branch), no call-site changes are needed — confirm this explicitly in the plan. If a new parameter is needed, list acli.py and acli_cli_ops.py as co-edit targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC 3's test scope to cover ALL behavior changes under narrowing: both codes removed from retry AND codes whose retry mechanism changes (502/503 gaining Retry-After/backoff logic).
findings.finding: AC 3 uses the phrase 'non-429 exit-code behavior' to describe what must be tested on a narrowing, but narrowing from 'all non-zero' to '{429,502,503}' also changes 502 and 503 treatment (they go from 'retry as generic non-zero' to 'retry with Retry-After/backoff logic'), so 'non-429' understates the scope of the behavior change under test.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword AC 3's test clause: 'if narrowed, tests cover (a) exit codes no longer retried and (b) changed retry mechanism for 502/503.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the probe specification (Step 1) to require capturing the **full raw stderr text** on a 429 response and verifying that the `Retry-After` numeric value (not just the header name) is present and extractable by the proposed regex — record a sample stderr snippet in the ticket findings.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `Retry-After` can be extracted from ACLI subprocess stderr via the regex `Retry-After:\s*(\d+)`, but this is an unverified capability assertion about the ACLI binary's stderr format. The probe (Step 1) is scoped to record only (a) whether Retry-After appears and (b) the exit code — it does not require verifying that the stderr format is stable, machine-parseable, or consistent across ACLI versions/error types. ACLI is a JVM/CLI tool; HTTP response headers are typically not echoed verbatim to stderr, and if the format varies (e.g., 'HTTP 429: Too Many Requests' without the Retry-After value), the regex silently returns nothing and the plan's conditional logic (`honors Retry-After IFF probe confirmed availability`) may give false confidence.
findings.location: What / files — Step 2: 'extract via regex from stderr (Retry-After:\s*(\d+))'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend Step 1 to: 'Record the complete raw stderr text on a 429; confirm the regex `Retry-After:\s*(\d+)` matches and extracts the delay value. If only the header name appears without a value, treat as unavailable.' Add an AC item: 'Probe records a sample stderr excerpt confirming numeric Retry-After extraction OR explicitly records it as absent/unparseable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to step 4 in the body explicitly instructing implementers to add a test case to `test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py` for the 429 retry path interacting with the non-idempotent-write guard.
findings.finding: AC 4 mandates a specific test-file action ('add a case to the EXISTING tests/.../test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py') that has no corresponding instruction in the plan body's step 4, which only says 'PRESERVE the non-idempotent-write guard.'
findings.suggested_fix: Step 4 body addition: 'Add a test case to the existing `tests/.../test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py` verifying that the new 429 backoff path does not bypass the non-idempotent-write guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the pre-committed regex pattern from Step 2 and instead derive the extraction mechanism (regex, structured parse, or 'unavailable') FROM the probe outcome; the implementation choice should be a documented post-probe decision, not a pre-probe specification.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a specific regex extraction mechanism (`Retry-After:\s*(\d+)`) from subprocess stderr before the probe (Step 1) has confirmed ACLI even surfaces this header in its output. This is cargo-cult application of the REST-path pattern (`exc.headers.get('Retry-After')`) to a subprocess text-output context where the mechanism is unconfirmed.
findings.location: What/files section, Step 2: 'extract via regex from stderr (`Retry-After:\s*(\d+)`) and honor it'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test case (in `test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py`) for a 429 response where `Retry-After` is present in stderr but non-numeric/unparseable, verifying the implementation falls back to jittered exponential backoff rather than erroring out or using a zero/negative delay.
findings.finding: The testing plan directs adding a 'case' to test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py but does not address the failure/boundary path where the Retry-After header value is present but malformed or non-numeric (e.g., 'Retry-After: soon', 'Retry-After: ', or regex parse failure), leaving the fallback behavior in that branch unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 2: '429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff; honors Retry-After IFF the probe confirmed availability.' / Step 2: 'extract via regex from stderr (`Retry-After:\s*(\d+)`)' / AC item 4: 'add a case to the EXISTING tests/.../test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC 2 or AC 4, add: 'A test case covers the malformed-Retry-After scenario: stderr contains `Retry-After:` but with a non-numeric value; assert the implementation falls back to jittered backoff and does not raise an uncaught exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to the add-on approach (as recommended) in the plan text, removing the open 'Decide' branch; if narrowing is ever wanted, gate it on a separate ticket.
findings.finding: Step 3 defers the add-on-vs-narrow retryable-code decision to the executor despite having a stated recommendation, making it a placeholder rather than a committed design choice.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Decide + document: ADD ... (recommended) vs NARROW ...' with 'ADD selective 429/502/503 Retry-After/backoff ON TOP of the existing non-zero-exit-code retry logic (no narrowing); narrowing to {429,502,503} is out of scope for this ticket and requires a dedicated change with explicit tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the probe step into a post-implementation integration check) that runs the final implementation against a live or staged Jira endpoint, triggers a real 429, and asserts the backoff/Retry-After behavior observable in logs — or explicitly document rationale for deferring this live check to a named follow-on ticket.
findings.finding: The new 429-handling code path has no end-to-end acceptance criterion that exercises it against a real/live Jira 429 response after implementation; the only live exercise is the pre-implementation probe (Step 1), which verifies baseline ACLI behavior, not the final implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs) / What — Step 1 vs post-implementation verification

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe AC1 as a durable artifact: e.g. 'A documented probe result (exit code + Retry-After availability) exists in the ticket or a linked note, AND the implementation explicitly references which branch (Retry-After available / unavailable) was chosen based on those findings.'
findings.finding: AC1 ('Probe result recorded') is effort-framed and describes a one-time process transition, not a durable observable system outcome, making it unmeasurable as a system-state criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If the narrowing option is kept open, add an explicit back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag, an expand-contract sequence, or a one-line revert instruction) to step 3 and the ADR referenced in AC 6.
findings.finding: The plan leaves the 'NARROW to {429,502,503}' option open as a valid choice but provides no rollback or back-out plan if narrowing is chosen; narrowing is a breaking behavioral change (callers that relied on retrying arbitrary non-zero exit codes lose that behavior), and T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not merely acknowledgement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to step 3: 'If narrowing is chosen, document a rollback procedure (e.g., revert the exit-code allowlist to "all non-zero" via a one-line config change or feature flag) and include it in the ADR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether backoff parameters (initial delay, max delay, jitter range, max retries) are named module-level constants or configurable — and document the chosen defaults with a rationale derived from the probe results.
findings.finding: The plan introduces jittered exponential backoff with no statement about whether backoff parameters (initial delay, max delay, jitter range, max retry count) will be configurable or hardcoded constants — these are operationally significant thresholds that teams typically want to tune.
findings.location: What/files section step 2; Acceptance Criteria item 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: 'Backoff parameters (initial_delay, max_delay, jitter_range, max_retries) are defined as named constants (or config-driven) rather than inline magic numbers; default values are justified by probe observations or documented as conservative starting points.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
